Frrn Polrrrclr- Pnrcrrcps C ovtvrtssloN
P.O. Box lt07 . -ll8 J Street . Sacmnrento. CA q5Sll-01t07
(916) 321-5660 . Fax (916) i22{)Utt6
May 2,2000
Michael A. Ramos, D.D.A.
Major Crimes Unit
County of San Bernardino
Office of the District Attorney
316 North Mountain View
San Bernardino, Califomia 92415-0004
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No.I-00-079
Dear Mr. Ramos
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").r Since your questions do not pertain to a particular
governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.2
QUESTTONS
l. Does the Act restrict your wife from accepting employment as a substifute teacher
with the school district?
2. If your wife accepts employment as a teacher with the school district, may you
participate in decisions concerning teacher contracts?
CONCLUSIONS
1. No. The Act does not prohibit your wife from accepting employment as a substitute
teacher with the school district.
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83 I 14; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


File No. I-00-079
Page 2
2. You may participate in school board decisions pertaining to teacher contracts provided
there is no personal financial effect on you or your immediate family.
F'ACTS
You are a deputy district attorney for San Bemardino County. You are also a school
board member for the Redlands Unified School District (the "school district').
Your wife is currently working on her teaching credential. She would like to teach in
your school district. The board votes annually on teacher contracts that apply to all employees
equally. You would not vote on employment or other matters regarding your wife individually
ANALYSIS
A. Introduction.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions enfllre that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. As a board member of the Redlands Unified School District, you are a public
official for purposes of the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701.)
The conflict-of-interest analysis under the Act is a four-part test: (1) Apublic fficial
must be making, participating in making, or using his or her fficial position to influence a
governmental decision, (2) and it must be reasonablyforeseeable that, (3) the decision will have
amaterialfinancial ffict, (4) distinguishablefrom the effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of the official's immediate family, or on any one of five stafutorily identified
economic interests of the official.
You will have a conflict of interest in a decision if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable3 and material financial effect on your economic interests which is distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally.
3 Wh"ther the financial consequences of a given goverunental decision are reqsonably foreseeable at the
time the decision is made is highly situation-specific. A financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered
reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to meet the standard. On the other hand, if
an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (ln re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)


File No. I-00-079
Page 3
B. Making, participating in making, or using official position to influence
governmental decisions.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where the public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use hisofficial posilion to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest."
(Section 87100.)
A public official "makes a governmental decision," when the official, among other
things, votes on a matter. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a
govemmental decision," when he or she, among others things, advises or makes
recortmendations to the decisionmaker either directly or without significant intervening
substantive review, by conducting research or making any investigation that requires the exercise
ofjudgment on the part of the official and the pu{pose of which is to influence a governmental
decision, or by preparing or presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally, or in writing, that
requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which is to
influence a governmental decision. (Regulation 15702.2.) Using his official position to
influence a decision includes contacts, appearances before, or otherwise attempting to influence
any member, officer, employee, or consultant of the agency. Attempts to influence include, but
are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer. (Regulation 187 02.3(a).)
C. Economic interests.
1. Inhoduction.
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts arising from economic
interests. Section 87103 recognizes five kinds of economic interests from which conflicts of
interest may arise:
A business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect investmenta of $1,000
or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a); or in which a public official is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87 1 03(d); Regulation 1 8703. I (b).)
a
a Section 87103 defines an 'indirect investnent' or interest as any investnent or interest owned by the
spouse or dependent child ofa public official, by an agent on behalfofa public official, or by a business entity or
trust in which the official, the official's agents, spouse, and dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficially a l0-percent interest or greater.


a
File No. I-00-079
Page 4
Real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect interest of $1,000 or more
(Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
Any source of income (or promised income) to the public official which aggregates to
$250 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation
18703.3.)
Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $3005 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18703.4.)
A public official also has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. (This is sometimes
known as the "personal financial effects" rule.) (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Presumably, the unified school district is not a disqualiffing economic interest of yours
2. Your soouse as an economic interest.
Regulation 18705(c)(1) provides that a public official need not disqualiff himself or
herself from a// decisions affecting his or her spouse's income if:
"(1) The decision only affects the salary, per diem, or
reimbursement for expenses the official or his or her spouse
receives from a state or local government agency. This subdivision
does not apply to decisions to hire, fire, promote, demote, or
discipline an official's spouse, or to set a salary for an official's
spouse which is different from salaries paid to other employees of
the spouse's agency in the same job classification or position; . . ."
A public offtcial's income, for purposes of the Act, includes his or her community
property interest in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Generally, a public
official's community property interest in his or her spouse's income would be considered income
to the public official, and the public official would have an economic interest in the source of
that income. (Ibid.; Section 87103(c).) However, salary and reimbursement for expenses from a
state, local, or federal govemment agency are expressly excluded from the Act's definition of
income. (Section 82030(bX2).)
I the gift limit amount is adjusted biennially by ttre Commission to reflect changes in the Consumer Price
Index. As of January 1,1999, the amount is $300. (Section 87103(e); Regulation 18940.2.)
o
a
a


File No. I-00-079
Page 5
Accordingly, the income your wife would receive as an employee of the unified school
district does not make the district a source of income to you because of the "government salary"
exception. Thus, your community property interest of your wife's income from the district does
not, by itsel{ present a conflict of interest that would prohibit you from taking part in school
board decisions that affect the district. In short, we have long advised that the mere fact that a
public official's spouse is employed by the same agency (or a related agency) does not in and of
itselfpresent a conflict. (See, e.g., Rowe Advice Letter, No. I-98-070; and Grffin Advice Letter,
No. I-98-012.)
D. Summary of advice.
Based upon this law, we advise that in and of itself the fact that you are a board member
of the Redlands Unified School District and your wife wishes to work as a substitute teacher for
the district, does not create a potentially disqualiffing conflict of interest under the Act.
However, you would be prohibited from making, participating in making, or using your official
position to influence decisions that will result in your spouse's hiring, firing, demotion,
discipline, or the setting of her salary to a level different from those paid to other employees in
the same job classification or position.
I would further advise that you may want to check with your district counsel to determine
if there are any other laws that may be applicable to your facts. Please note, that our advice is
limited to the Act. You may wish to contact the Attorney General's Office with respect to other
provisions of law, such as the law governing incompatible public offices and Government Code
Section 1090.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-s660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Consultant, Legal Divi sion

equally. You would not vote on employment or other matters regarding your wife individually

QUESTION: l. Does the Act restrict your wife from accepting employment as a substifute teacher
with the school district?
2. If your wife accepts employment as a teacher with the school district, may you
participate in decisions concerning teacher contracts?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. The Act does not prohibit your wife from accepting employment as a substitute
teacher with the school district.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83 I 14; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
2. You may participate in school board decisions pertaining to teacher contracts provided
there is no personal financial effect on you or your immediate family.
